text: Aluminium Tri-chloride as a working agent 42
A fast breeder reactor of 2000 MW(t) output using molten chlorides as fuel and coolant is discussed.
The possibility of using a dissociating gas as a secondary working agent in the turbine for example $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{Cl}_{6}$ and $\mathrm{N}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ is discussed.
Un surrégénérateur rôpide de 2000 MW(t) utilisant des chlorures fondus comme combustible et comme réfrigérant est décrit.
In addition it must be emphasized, that each breeder reactor, which is built to last 25-30 years, must be considered not only as a power producing device but also as a source of fissile material.
This type of reactor has been discussed for example by Nelson, (Argonne 1967) and Lane (USA 1970) especially as a high flux material testing fast reactor.

section: For Fast Breeder Reactors With Solid And Molten Fuel
text: These types have also been studied, in most cases using sodium as a coolant, (Nelson, Argonne 1967).
- thermal power 2050 MW(th) - 1936 MW(t) in core + 114 MW(t) in blanket giving
- electrical power 1000 MW(e) (in the most optimistic case)

The data given in table 1 obtained from these calculations are for a steady state reactor.
The detailed representation for the temperature distribution in a typical power reactor with a core output of 1936 MW(t) are given in fig.
7 (for a position 43 cm above the bottom of the core where the neutron flux is normalised to 1).
The bulk temperature of the fuel is here $998^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the temperature of the tube walls $857 - 839^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the bulk temperature of the coolant $781^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
For the total output of the core 1936 MW(t), the power distribution is as shown in fig.
9 which indicates the axial bulk temperature distribution in the fuel and in the coolant in the core.
The fuel bulk temperature changes form $980^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at the bottom to $965^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at 1/4 core height and is $998^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at 3/4 of core height.
The coolant temperature lies between $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ inlet and $793^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ outlet.

For a fuel velocity of $2 \, \text{m.s}^{-1}$ , when the coolant velocity falls from $12 \, \text{m.s}^{-1}$ to $1 \, \text{m.s}^{-1}$ the coolant outlet temperature increases from $784^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $893^{\circ}\text{C}$ for constant inlet temperature of $750^{\circ}\text{C}$ .
This change of coolant velocity and its bulk temperature results in the decrease of the mean core output from $2088 \, \text{MW(t)}$ to $598 \, \text{MW(t)}$ - that is approximately a factor 3!

The reference core with 1936 MW(t) coolant velocity $9 \, \text{m.s}^{-1}$ , fuel velocity $2 \, \text{m.s}^{-1}$ , tube pitch/dia ratio 1.15, has a plutonium inventory of 2252 kg of Pu (Pu-239 + Pu-240) which gives a specific power of 0.86 MW(t)/kg Pu.

- the doubling time for these reactors _s shorter than those for sodium cooled solid fuel reactors and, being equal to 7 years, gives a good doubling time.
- the mean burn up in the molten fuel, continuously reprocessed could be a factor 3-5 lower than that for the solid fuel system.

- a high power density $(\mathsf{MW}(\mathsf{t}) / \mathsf{m}^3$ of core) than the solid fuel reactor, which may decrease the capital costs of core, blanket and shielding, perhaps in the future an increasingly important part of power production costs.
Weinberg), that is for utilization of the dispersed uranium present in granites in amounts of the order of 10 ppm (the mean value of uranium concentration in the entire earths crust is 4 ppm).
$1 \, \text{m}^3$ of granite equals $2500 \, \text{kg}$ of minerals, and with a uranium content of $10 \, \text{ppm}$ we have $25 \, \text{g}$ of uranium per $1 \, \text{m}^3$ of granite.
As is known in the Swiss Alps in Piz-Giuv (Aar-Massif) the uranium content in the syenite equals 15 - 30 ppm (Prof.
Hügi 1971) so in this case for 30 ppm.
70 g Pu x 1 Mw d x 10³ kw x 24 x 3.6 x 10³ s = 6 x 10⁹ KJ
$1\mathrm{m}^3$ of petroleum product, with a specific weight $\mathrm{og}900\mathrm{kg / m}^3$ and heat of combustion $\sim 12000\mathrm{kCal / kg}$ gives:

In terms of volumes, $1 \, \text{m}^3$ of petroleum product is equivalent to $7 \, \text{dm}^3$ of granite (syenite) (or a cube $19 \, \text{cm} \times 19 \, \text{xm} \times 19 \, \text{cm}$ ) and in addition $1 \, \text{m}^3$ of oil requires approx. $3000 \, \text{kg}$ of oxygen or $10^{1000} \, \text{m}^3$ air for combustion.
In the future output of the Swiss nuclear power industry will reach 10'000 MW(e).
For this amount of electrical energy approx. $10^{\circ}000 \, \text{kg}$ Pu must be burnt annually.
In a steady state nuclear power industry this amount of plutonium could be obtained from $20^{\circ}000 \, \text{kg}$ of natural uranium (allowing for losses etc.).

So for the Swiss nuclear industry it would be sufficient to recover the uranium from approximately 1 million cubic meters of granite annually - in other words the volume of a tunnel $30 \, \text{m} \times 10 \, \text{m} \times 3000 \, \text{m}$ or an open mine $200 \times 200 \times 20 \, \text{m}$ .
In the future, when uranium is recovered from granites with only 0.001% (10 ppm) uranium the price of uranium may increase to 500 $\mathbb{B} / \mathbb{kg}$ that is 25 times.
Or in other words an increase in the cost of recovery of natural uranium by a factor of 25 will only raise the plutonium price from $8000\$/kg$ to $9000\$/kg$ .
- From a resource point of view, the energy content (per $\mathfrak{m}^3$ ) of granite is greatly superior to say fuel oil (130 times).
